content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 23, 2016

Michael C. Ghizzoni
County Counsel
105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 20 I
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-028

Dear Mr. Ghizzoni:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Is Supervisor Wolf prohibited from discussing constituent complaints with appropriate County departments when such complaints could result in enforcement actions against the developer of a project located within 75 feet of her home?

2. If Supervisor Wolf is prohibited from discussing constituent complaints related to the development located within 75 feet of her home, may her Chief of Staff handle the complaints instead?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes, Supervisor Wolf is prohibited from discussing complaints from constituents with appropriate County departments regarding the development located within 75 feet of her property because they are inextricably interrelated to a decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her financial interests.

2. However, her Chief of Staff would not be prohibited from discussing them with appropriate County staff.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-028
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the County Counsel for the County of Santa Barbara, and are requesting formal
advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. You are
requesting advice on how Supervisor Wolf and her Chief of Staff may handle complaints that they
receive from residents of Supervisor Wolf's district concerning the "The Boulders" residential
subdivision project. These complaints might lead to enforcement actions by the County.

Supervisor Wolf lives across the street from the closest corner of "The Boulders," which is
about 75 feet away. She does not have any other personal connection to the project. Supervisor
Wolf's Chief of Staff Ms. O'Gorman lives about five miles away and has no personal connection to
the project.

On January 9, 2013, the Board of Supervisors for the County of Santa Barbara approved the
revised Park Hill Estates Residential Subdivision Project (now called The Boulders). The Board of
Supervisors Agenda Letter for that action accurately described the project as consisting of the
subdivision of a 14.87-acre property into 16 residential lots, one common open space lot for a
detention basin, and one lot covering an existing private road. Supervisor Wolf recused herself from
hearing and voting on that project.

The project has begun construction, which has yielded some complaints to the County by
neighbors of the project. These complaints could involve The Boulders not complying with the
project's conditions of approval. If the developer is not complying with the project's conditions of
approval, that noncompliance might be subject to enforcement actions by the County. You would
like to know if Supervisor Wolf may receive those complaints and discuss those complaints with
appropriate County departments. If she is prohibited from doing so, could her Chief of Staff Mary
O'Gorman receive and discuss those complaints with appropriate County departments.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a disqualifying financial interest if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103 defines interests from which a conflict of interest may arise
to include any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

A public official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he
or she: (1) contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency subject to the
authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision; or (2)
contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for the purpose of affecting
a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of
his or her agency in making the contact. (Regulation 18704.)

File No. A-16-028
Page No. 3

Note that the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from receiving any communications from
constituents, but does place restrictions on the supervisor's communication to other departments
where a conflict of interest exists. Supervisor Wolf would be using her official position to influence
a governmental decision if she were to contact County departments to discuss those complaints.

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Real property is the subject of a decision when "the decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6)."

Here Supervisor Wolf's real property is not the subject of the decision or a named party in
the decisions, therefore her financial interest is not explicitly involved. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the
interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect reasonably foreseeable if the
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.
(Regulation 18701(b).)

The financial effect of a governmental decision is material whenever the governmental
decision would affect real property value located within 500 feet of the property line of the
official's real property (Regulation 18702.2). You have already determined that the Supervisor had a
conflict of interest in the development decision due to the proximity of the subject property to her
residence. The question here is whether communicating with the county concerning complaints
about the progress of the same development are somehow segmentable from the development
decision itself.

Regulation 18706 provides that related decisions are not segmentable if they are inextricably
interrelated. Regulation 18706(b) states:

"For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated'
when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify,
or alter the result of another decision."

There is a realistic possibility that if Supervisor Wolf discusses complaints about the project
with County officials the original decisions made about the development could be altered or
affirmed. Therefore, because the issues and decisions are interrelated, Supervisor Wolf has a
conflict as to both the development decision and resulting complaints.

Chief of Staff, Mary O'Gorman would not be prohibited from taking and discussing those
complaints with appropriate County departments as she does not have any financial interests that
could be affected. Moreover, the Supervisor's conflict of interest would not be imputed to her staff.

File No. A-16-028
Page No. 4

of course the Supervisor cannot influence the decisions through her staff in which she has a conflict of interest, and should be walled off from any involvement.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By:    Sukhi K. Brar
       Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl
